title: The Body - Value Without Pitching Quiz

questions.question: What is the primary mistake solo founders make in the body of a cold email?

options.text: Using too much white space
Treating the email body like a product brochure or listing features
Including their contact information
Not including enough images

questions.explanation: Prospects are busy and don't care about your technical specs yet. They care about their problems and potential outcomes.

questions.question: What is 'Outcome Positioning'?

options.text: Describing exactly how the software was built
Focusing on what the customer can DO or ACHIEVE with your product, rather than what the product is
Positioning your product as the cheapest in the market
Adding a list of testimonials at the bottom

questions.explanation: Outcomes (e.g., 'Safe 10 hours a week') are the reason people buy. Features (e.g., 'AI-powered database') are secondary.

questions.question: How do you establish 'Credibility Without Desperation'?

options.text: By claiming you are 'The Best' or 'Number One'
By sending the email multiple times a day
By sharing specific, relevant results or social proof ('We helped X do Y')
By offering a deep discount in the first email

questions.explanation: Specific results speak for themselves. Vague claims of greatness sound desperate and marketing-driven.

questions.question: Why is 'Scanning' important in cold email design?

options.text: It helps the email get through spam filters
Most prospects won't read every word; they will skim the text to see if it's worth their time
It allows you to use smaller fonts
It is required for accessibility

questions.explanation: The human brain scans for relevance. If you use long paragraphs, the important information (the outcome) gets lost and the email is deleted.

questions.question: What is the 'Golden Rule' for translating features into outcomes?

options.text: Ask 'So what?' after every feature you write
Always use technical jargon to show your intelligence
Make the feature first and the outcome second
Never mention the outcome; let the customer guess it

questions.explanation: Asking 'So what?' forces you to identify the actual business value of a technical feature. For example: 'We have a fast API.' (So what?) 'Your devs spend zero time waiting for data to load.'

questions.question: Take one technical feature of your product. Perform the 'So What?' analysis 3 times to get to the core outcome. Draft a 2-sentence value proposition based on that final outcome. Does it sound more compelling than the feature list?
questions.promptForAI: Evaluate if the founder moves from technical specs to business results. The final value prop should address a pain point or transformation.
